units.text: My friends,—

units.speaker: FIRST OUTLAW
units.text: That's not so, sir: we are your enemies.

units.speaker: SECOND OUTLAW
units.text: Peace! We'll hear him.

units.speaker: THIRD OUTLAW
units.text: Ay, by my beard, will we, for he's a proper man.

units.speaker: VALENTINE
units.text:
Then know that I have little wealth to lose:
A man I am cross'd with adversity;
My riches are these poor habiliments,
Of which if you should here disfurnish me,
You take the sum and substance that I have.

units.speaker: SECOND OUTLAW
units.text: Whither travel you?

units.speaker: VALENTINE
units.text: To Verona.

units.speaker: FIRST OUTLAW
units.text: Whence came you?

units.speaker: VALENTINE
units.text: From Milan.

units.speaker: THIRD OUTLAW
units.text: Have you long sojourned there?

units.speaker: VALENTINE
units.text:
Some sixteen months, and longer might have stay'd,
If crooked fortune had not thwarted me.

units.speaker: FIRST OUTLAW
units.text: What, were you banish'd thence?

units.speaker: VALENTINE
units.text: I was.

units.speaker: SECOND OUTLAW
units.text: For what offence?

units.speaker: VALENTINE
units.text:
For that which now torments me to rehearse:
I kill'd a man, whose death I much repent;
But yet I slew him manfully in fight,
Without false vantage or base treachery.

units.speaker: FIRST OUTLAW
units.text:
Why, ne'er repent it, if it were done so.
But were you banish'd for so small a fault?

units.speaker: VALENTINE
units.text: I was, and held me glad of such a doom.

units.speaker: SECOND OUTLAW
units.text: Have you the tongues?

units.speaker: VALENTINE
units.text:
My youthful travel therein made me happy,
Or else I often had been miserable.

units.speaker: THIRD OUTLAW
units.text:
By the bare scalp of Robin Hood's fat friar,
This fellow were a king for our wild faction!

units.speaker: FIRST OUTLAW
units.text: We'll have him. Sirs, a word.

units.speaker: SPEED
units.text:
Master, be one of them; it's an honourable kind of
thievery.

units.speaker: VALENTINE
units.text: Peace, villain!

units.speaker: SECOND OUTLAW
units.text: Tell us this: have you any thing to take to?

units.speaker: VALENTINE
units.text: Nothing but my fortune.

units.speaker: THIRD OUTLAW
units.text:
Know, then, that some of us are gentlemen,
Such as the fury of ungovern'd youth
Thrust from the company of awful men:
Myself was from Verona banished
For practising to steal away a lady,
An heir, and near allied unto the duke.

units.speaker: SECOND OUTLAW
units.text:
And I from Mantua, for a gentleman,
Who, in my mood, I stabb'd unto the heart.

units.speaker: FIRST OUTLAW
units.text:
And I for such like petty crimes as these,
But to the purpose—for we cite our faults,
That they may hold excus'd our lawless lives;
And partly, seeing you are beautified
With goodly shape and by your own report
A linguist and a man of such perfection
As we do in our quality much want—

units.speaker: SECOND OUTLAW
units.text:
Indeed, because you are a banish'd man,
Therefore, above the rest, we parley to you:
Are you content to be our general?
To make a virtue of necessity
And live, as we do, in this wilderness?

units.speaker: THIRD OUTLAW
units.text:
What say'st thou? Wilt thou be of our consort?
Say ay, and be the captain of us all:
We'll do thee homage and be ruled by thee,
Love thee as our commander and our king.

units.speaker: FIRST OUTLAW
units.text: But if thou scorn our courtesy, thou diest.

units.speaker: SECOND OUTLAW
units.text: Thou shalt not live to brag what we have offer'd.

units.speaker: VALENTINE
units.text:
I take your offer and will live with you,
Provided that you do no outrages
On silly women or poor passengers.

units.speaker: THIRD OUTLAW
units.text:
No, we detest such vile base practises.
Come, go with us, we'll bring thee to our crews,
And show thee all the treasure we have got,
Which, with ourselves, all rest at thy dispose.
Exeunt
Enter PROTEUS

units.speaker: PROTEUS
units.text:
Already have I been false to Valentine
And now I must be as unjust to Turio.
Under the colour of commending him,
I have access my own love to prefer:
But Silvia is too fair, too true, too holy,
To be corrupted with my worthless gifts.
When I protest true loyalty to her,
She twits me with my falsehood to my friend;
When to her beauty I commend my vows,
She bids me think how I have been forsworn
In breaking faith with Julia whom I loved:
And notwithstanding all her sudden quips,
The least whereof would quell a lover's hope,
Yet, spaniel-like, the more she spurns my love,
The more it grows and fawneth on her still.
But here comes Turio: now must we to her window,
And give some evening music to her ear.
Enter TURIO and Musicians

units.speaker: TURIO
units.text: How now, Sir Proteus, are you crept before us?

units.speaker: PROTEUS
units.text:
Ay, gentle Turio: for you know that love
Will creep in service where it cannot go.

units.speaker: TURIO
units.text: Ay, but I hope, sir, that you love not here.

units.speaker: PROTEUS
units.text: Sir, but I do; or else I would be hence.

units.speaker: TURIO
units.text: Who? Silvia?

units.speaker: PROTEUS
units.text: Ay, Silvia; for your sake.

units.speaker: TURIO
units.text:
I thank you for your own. Now, gentlemen,
Let's tune, and to it lustily awhile.
Enter, at a distance, Host, and JULIA in boy's clothes

units.speaker: HOST
units.text:
Now, my young guest, methinks you're allycholly: I
pray you, why is it?

units.speaker: JULIA
units.text: Marry, mine host, because I cannot be merry.

units.speaker: HOST
units.text:
Come, we'll have you merry: I'll bring you where
you shall hear music and see the gentleman that you
asked for.

units.speaker: JULIA
units.text: But shall I hear him speak?

units.speaker: HOST
units.text: Ay, that you shall.

units.speaker: JULIA
units.text: That will be music.
Music plays

units.speaker: HOST
units.text: Hark, hark!

units.speaker: JULIA
units.text: Is he among these?

units.speaker: HOST
units.text: Ay: but, peace! Let's hear 'em.

units.speaker: HOST
units.text:
How now! Are you sadder than you were before? How
do you, man? The music likes you not.

units.speaker: JULIA
units.text: You mistake; the musician likes me not.

units.speaker: HOST
units.text: Why, my pretty youth?

units.speaker: JULIA
units.text: He plays false, father.

units.speaker: HOST
units.text: How? Out of tune on the strings?

units.speaker: JULIA
units.text:
Not so; but yet so false that he grieves my very
heart-strings.

units.speaker: HOST
units.text: You have a quick ear.

units.speaker: JULIA
units.text: Ay, I would I were deaf; it makes me have a slow heart.

units.speaker: HOST
units.text: I perceive you delight not in music.

units.speaker: JULIA
units.text: Not a whit, when it jars so.

units.speaker: HOST
units.text: Hark, what fine change is in the music!

units.speaker: JULIA
units.text: Ay, that change is the spite.

units.speaker: HOST
units.text: You would have them always play but one thing?

units.speaker: JULIA
units.text:
I would always have one play but one thing.
But, host, doth this Sir Proteus that we talk on
Often resort unto this gentlewoman?

units.speaker: HOST
units.text:
I tell you what Lance, his man, told me: he loved
her out of all nick.

units.speaker: JULIA
units.text: Where is Lance?

units.speaker: HOST
units.text:
Gone to seek his dog; which tomorrow, by his
master's command, he must carry for a present to his
lady.

units.speaker: JULIA
units.text: Peace! Stand aside: the company parts.

units.speaker: PROTEUS
units.text:
Sir Turio, fear not you: I will so plead
That you shall say my cunning drift excels.

units.speaker: TURIO
units.text: Where meet we?

units.speaker: PROTEUS
units.text: At Saint Gregory's well.

units.speaker: TURIO
units.text: Farewell.
Exeunt TURIO and Musicians
Enter SILVIA above.

units.speaker: PROTEUS
units.text: Madam, good even to your ladyship.

units.speaker: SILVIA
units.text:
I thank you for your music, gentlemen.
Who is that that spake?

units.speaker: PROTEUS
units.text:
One, lady, if you knew his pure heart's truth,
You would quickly learn to know him by his voice.

units.speaker: SILVIA
units.text: Sir Proteus, as I take it.

units.speaker: PROTEUS
units.text: Sir Proteus, gentle lady, and your servant.

units.speaker: SILVIA
units.text: What's your will?

units.speaker: PROTEUS
units.text: That I may compass yours.

units.speaker: SILVIA
units.text:
You have your wish; my will is even this:
That presently you hie you home to bed.
Thou subtle, perjured, false, disloyal man!
Think'st thou I am so shallow, so conceitless,
To be seduced by thy flattery,
That hast deceived so many with thy vows?
Return, return, and make thy love amends.
For me, by this pale queen of night I swear,
I am so far from granting thy request
That I despise thee for thy wrongful suit,
And by and by intend to chide myself
Even for this time I spend in talking to thee.

units.speaker: PROTEUS
units.text:
I grant, sweet love, that I did love a lady;
But she is dead.

units.speaker: JULIA
units.text:
[Aside] 'Twere false, if I should speak it;
For I am sure she is not buried.

units.speaker: SILVIA
units.text:
Say that she be; yet Valentine thy friend
Survives; to whom, thyself art witness,
I am betroth'd : and art thou not ashamed
To wrong him with thy importunacy?

units.speaker: PROTEUS
units.text: I likewise hear that Valentine is dead.

units.speaker: SILVIA
units.text:
And so suppose am I; for in his grave
Assure thyself my love is buried.

units.speaker: PROTEUS
units.text: Sweet lady, let me rake it from the earth.

units.speaker: SILVIA
units.text:
Go to thy lady's grave and call hers thence,
Or, at the least, in hers sepulchre thine.

units.speaker: JULIA
units.text: [Aside] He heard not that.

units.speaker: PROTEUS
units.text:
Madam, if your heart be so obdurate,
Vouchsafe me yet your picture for my love,
The picture that is hanging in your chamber;
To that I'll speak, to that I'll sigh and weep:
For since the substance of your perfect self
Is else devoted, I am but a shadow;
And to your shadow will I make true love.

units.speaker: JULIA
units.text:
[Aside] If 'twere a substance, you would, sure,
deceive it,
And make it but a shadow, as I am.

units.speaker: SILVIA
units.text:
I am very loath to be your idol, sir;
But since your falsehood shall become you well
To worship shadows and adore false shapes,
Send to me in the morning and I'll send it:
And so, good rest.

units.speaker: PROTEUS
units.text:
As wretches have o'ernight
That wait for execution in the morn.
Exeunt PROTEUS and SILVIA severally

units.speaker: JULIA
units.text: Host, will you go?

units.speaker: HOST
units.text: By my halidom, I was fast asleep.

units.speaker: JULIA
units.text: Pray you, where lies Sir Proteus?

units.speaker: HOST
units.text:
Marry, at my house. Trust me, I think 'tis almost
day.

units.speaker: JULIA
units.text:
Not so; but it hath been the longest night
That e'er I watch'd and the most heaviest.
Exeunt
Enter EGLAMOUR

units.speaker: EGLAMOUR
units.text:
This is the hour that Madam Silvia
Entreated me to call and know her mind:
There's some great matter she'ld employ me in.
Madam, madam!
Enter SILVIA above

units.speaker: SILVIA
units.text: Who calls?

units.speaker: EGLAMOUR
units.text:
Your servant and your friend;
One that attends your ladyship's command.

units.speaker: SILVIA
units.text: Sir Eglamour, a thousand times good morrow.

units.speaker: EGLAMOUR
units.text:
As many, worthy lady, to yourself:
According to your ladyship's impose,
I am thus early come to know what service
It is your pleasure to command me in.

units.speaker: SILVIA
units.text:
O Eglamour, thou art a gentleman—
Think not I flatter, for I swear I do not—
Valiant, wise, remorseful, well accomplish'd:
Thou art not ignorant what dear good will
I bear unto the banish'd Valentine,
Nor how my father would enforce me marry
Vain Turio, whom my very soul abhors.
Thyself hast loved; and I have heard thee say
No grief did ever come so near thy heart
As when thy lady and thy true love died,
Upon whose grave thou vow'dst pure chastity.
Sir Eglamour, I would to Valentine,
To Mantua, where I hear he makes abode;
And, for the ways are dangerous to pass,
I do desire thy worthy company,
Upon whose faith and honour I repose.
Urge not my father's anger, Eglamour,
But think upon my grief, a lady's grief,
And on the justice of my flying hence,
To keep me from a most unholy match,
Which heaven and fortune still rewards with plagues.
I do desire thee, even from a heart
As full of sorrows as the sea of sands,
To bear me company and go with me:
If not, to hide what I have said to thee,
That I may venture to depart alone.

units.speaker: EGLAMOUR
units.text:
Madam, I pity much your grievances;
Which since I know they virtuously are placed,
I give consent to go along with you,
Recking as little what betideth me
As much I wish all good befortune you.
When will you go?

units.speaker: SILVIA
units.text: This evening coming.

units.speaker: EGLAMOUR
units.text: Where shall I meet you?

units.speaker: SILVIA
units.text:
At Friar Patrick's cell,
Where I intend holy confession.

units.speaker: EGLAMOUR
units.text:
I will not fail your ladyship. Good morrow, gentle
lady.

units.speaker: SILVIA
units.text: Good morrow, kind Sir Eglamour.
Exeunt severally
Enter LANCE, with his his Dog

units.speaker: LANCE
units.text:
When a man's servant shall play the cur with him,
look you, it goes hard: one that I brought up of a
puppy; one that I saved from drowning, when three or
four of his blind brothers and sisters went to it.
I have taught him, even as one would say precisely,
'thus I would teach a dog.' I was sent to deliver
him as a present to Mistress Silvia from my master;
and I came no sooner into the dining-chamber but he
steps me to her trencher and steals her capon's leg:
O, 'tis a foul thing when a cur cannot keep himself
in all companies! I would have, as one should say,
one that takes upon him to be a dog indeed, to be,
as it were, a dog at all things. If I had not had
more wit than he, to take a fault upon me that he did,
I think verily he had been hanged for't; sure as I
live, he had suffered for't; you shall judge. He
thrusts me himself into the company of three or four
gentlemanlike dogs under the duke's table: he had
not been there—bless the mark!—a pissing while, but
all the chamber smelt him. 'Out with the dog!' says
one: 'What cur is that?' says another: 'Whip him
out' says the third: 'Hang him up' says the duke.
I, having been acquainted with the smell before,
knew it was Crab, and goes me to the fellow that
whips the dogs: 'Friend,' quoth I, 'you mean to whip
the dog?' 'Ay, marry, do I,' quoth he. 'You do him
the more wrong,' quoth I; ''twas I did the thing you
wot of.' He makes me no more ado, but whips me out
of the chamber. How many masters would do this for
his servant? Nay, I'll be sworn, I have sat in the
stocks for puddings he hath stolen, otherwise he had
been executed; I have stood on the pillory for geese
he hath killed, otherwise he had suffered for't.
Thou thinkest not of this now. Nay, I remember the
trick you served me when I took my leave of Madam
Silvia: did not I bid thee still mark me and do as I
do? When didst thou see me heave up my leg and make
water against a gentlewoman's farthingale? Didst
thou ever see me do such a trick?
Enter PROTEUS and JULIA

units.speaker: PROTEUS
units.text:
Sebastian is thy name? I like thee well
And will employ thee in some service presently.

units.speaker: JULIA
units.text: In what you please: I'll do what I can.

units.speaker: PROTEUS
units.text: I hope thou wilt.

units.speaker: PROTEUS
units.text:
[to LANCE] How now, you whoreson peasant!
Where have you been these two days loitering?

units.speaker: LANCE
units.text:
Marry, sir, I carried Mistress Silvia the dog you bade
me.

units.speaker: PROTEUS
units.text: And what says she to my little jewel?

units.speaker: LANCE
units.text:
Marry, she says your dog was a cur, and tells you
currish thanks is good enough for such a present.

units.speaker: PROTEUS
units.text: But she received my dog?

units.speaker: LANCE
units.text:
No, indeed, did she not: here have I brought him
back again.

units.speaker: PROTEUS
units.text: What, didst thou offer her this from me?

units.speaker: LANCE
units.text:
Ay, sir: the other squirrel was stolen from me by
the hangman boys in the market-place: and then I
offered her mine own, who is a dog as big as ten of
yours, and therefore the gift the greater.

units.speaker: PROTEUS
units.text:
Go get thee hence, and find my dog again,
Or ne'er return again into my sight.
Away, I say! Stay'st thou to vex me here?
Exit LANCE

units.speaker: PROTEUS
units.text:
A slave, that still an end turns me to shame!
Sebastian, I have entertained thee,
Partly that I have need of such a youth
That can with some discretion do my business,
For 'tis no trusting to yond foolish lout,
But chiefly for thy face and thy behavior,
Which, if my augury deceive me not,
Witness good bringing up, fortune and truth:
Therefore know thou, for this I entertain thee.
Go presently and take this ring with thee,
Deliver it to Madam Silvia:
She loved me well deliver'd it to me.

units.speaker: JULIA
units.text:
It seems you loved not her, to leave her token.
She is dead, belike?

units.speaker: PROTEUS
units.text: Not so; I think she lives.

units.speaker: JULIA
units.text: Alas!

units.speaker: PROTEUS
units.text: Why dost thou cry 'alas'?

units.speaker: JULIA
units.text:
I cannot choose
But pity her.

units.speaker: PROTEUS
units.text: Wherefore shouldst thou pity her?

units.speaker: JULIA
PROTEUS
units.text:
Well, give her that ring and therewithal
This letter. That's her chamber. Tell my lady
I claim the promise for her heavenly picture.
Your message done, hie home unto my chamber,
Where thou shalt find me, sad and solitary.
Exit

units.speaker: JULIA
units.text:
How many women would do such a message?
Alas, poor Proteus! Thou hast entertain'd
A fox to be the shepherd of thy lambs.
Alas, poor fool! Why do I pity him
That with his very heart despiseth me?
Because he loves her, he despiseth me;
Because I love him I must pity him.
This ring I gave him when he parted from me,
To bind him to remember my good will;
And now am I, unhappy messenger,
To plead for that which I would not obtain,
To carry that which I would have refused,
To praise his faith which I would have dispraised.
I am my master's true-confirmed love;
But cannot be true servant to my master,
Unless I prove false traitor to myself.
Yet will I woo for him, but yet so coldly
As, heaven it knows, I would not have him speed.
Enter SILVIA, attended

units.speaker: JULIA
units.text:
Gentlewoman, good day! I pray you, be my mean
To bring me where to speak with Madam Silvia.

units.speaker: SILVIA
units.text: What would you with her, if that I be she?

units.speaker: JULIA
units.text:
If you be she, I do entreat your patience
To hear me speak the message I am sent on.

units.speaker: SILVIA
units.text: From whom?

units.speaker: JULIA
units.text: From my master, Sir Proteus, madam.

units.speaker: SILVIA
units.text: O, he sends you for a picture.

units.speaker: JULIA
units.text: Ay, madam.

units.speaker: SILVIA
units.text:
Ursula, bring my picture here.
Go give your master this: tell him from me,
One Julia, that his changing thoughts forget,
Would better fit his chamber than this shadow.

units.speaker: JULIA
units.text:
Madam, please you peruse this letter.—
Pardon me, madam; I have unadvised
Deliver'd you a paper that I should not:
This is the letter to your ladyship.

units.speaker: SILVIA
units.text: I pray thee, let me look on that again.

units.speaker: JULIA
units.text: It may not be; good madam, pardon me.

units.speaker: SILVIA
units.text:
There, hold!
I will not look upon your master's lines:
I know they are stuff'd with protestations
And full of new-found oaths; which he will break
As easily as I do tear his paper.

units.speaker: JULIA
units.text: Madam, he sends your ladyship this ring.

units.speaker: SILVIA
units.text:
The more shame for him that he sends it me;
For I have heard him say a thousand times
His Julia gave it him at his departure.
Though his false finger have profaned the ring,
Mine shall not do his Julia so much wrong.

units.speaker: JULIA
units.text: She thanks you.

units.speaker: SILVIA
units.text: What say'st thou?

units.speaker: JULIA
units.text:
I thank you, madam, that you tender her.
Poor gentlewoman! My master wrongs her much.

units.speaker: SILVIA
units.text: Dost thou know her?

units.speaker: JULIA
units.text:
Almost as well as I do know myself:
To think upon her woes I do protest
That I have wept a hundred several times.

units.speaker: SILVIA
units.text: Belike she thinks that Proteus hath forsook her.

units.speaker: JULIA
units.text: I think she doth; and that's her cause of sorrow.

units.speaker: SILVIA
units.text: Is she not passing fair?

units.speaker: JULIA
units.text:
She hath been fairer, madam, than she is:
When she did think my master loved her well,
She, in my judgment, was as fair as you:
But since she did neglect her looking-glass
And threw her sun-expelling mask away,
The air hath starved the roses in her cheeks
And pinch'd the lily-tincture of her face,
That now she is become as black as I.

units.speaker: SILVIA
units.text: How tall was she?

units.speaker: JULIA
units.text:
About my stature; for at Pentecost,
When all our pageants of delight were play'd,
Our youth got me to play the woman's part,
And I was trimm'd in Madam Julia's gown,
Which served me as fit, by all men's judgments,
As if the garment had been made for me:
Therefore I know she is about my height.
And at that time I made her weep agood,
For I did play a lamentable part:
Madam, 'twas Ariadne passioning
For Theseus' perjury and unjust flight;
Which I so lively acted with my tears
That my poor mistress, moved therewithal,
Wept bitterly; and would I might be dead
If I in thought felt not her very sorrow!

units.speaker: SILVIA
units.text:
She is beholding to thee, gentle youth.
Alas, poor lady, desolate and left!
I weep myself to think upon thy words.
Here, youth, there is my purse; I give thee this
For thy sweet mistress' sake, because thou lovest her.
Farewell.
Exit SILVIA, with attendants

units.speaker: JULIA
units.text:
And she shall thank you for't, if e'er you know her.
A virtuous gentlewoman, mild and beautiful
I hope my master's suit will be but cold,
Since she respects my mistress' love so much.
Alas, how love can trifle with itself!
Here is her picture: let me see; I think,
If I had such a tire, this face of mine
Were full as lovely as is this of hers:
And yet the painter flatter'd her a little,
Unless I flatter with myself too much.
Her hair is auburn, mine is perfect yellow:
If that be all the difference in his love,
I'll get me such a colour'd periwig.
Her eyes are grey as glass, and so are mine:
Ay, but her forehead's low, and mine's as high.
What should it be that he respects in her
But I can make respective in myself,
If this fond Love were not a blinded god?
Come, shadow, come and take this shadow up,
For 'tis thy rival. O thou senseless form,
Thou shalt be worshipp'd, kiss'd, loved and adored!
And, were there sense in his idolatry,
My substance should be statue in thy stead.
I'll use thee kindly for thy mistress' sake,
That used me so; or else, by Jove I vow,
I should have scratch'd out your unseeing eyes
To make my master out of love with thee!
Exit
Enter EGLAMOUR

units.speaker: EGLAMOUR
units.text:
The sun begins to gild the western sky;
And now it is about the very hour
That Silvia, at Friar Patrick's cell, should meet me.
She will not fail, for lovers break not hours,
Unless it be to come before their time;
So much they spur their expedition.
See where she comes.
Enter SILVIA

units.speaker: EGLAMOUR
units.text: Lady, a happy evening!

units.speaker: SILVIA
units.text:
Amen, amen! Go on, good Eglamour,
Out at the postern by the abbey-wall:
I fear I am attended by some spies.

units.speaker: EGLAMOUR
units.text:
Fear not: the forest is not three leagues off;
If we recover that, we are sure enough.
Exeunt
Enter TURIO, PROTEUS, and JULIA

units.speaker: TURIO
units.text: Sir Proteus, what says Silvia to my suit?

units.speaker: PROTEUS
units.text:
O, sir, I find her milder than she was;
And yet she takes exceptions at your person.

units.speaker: TURIO
units.text: What, that my leg is too long?

units.speaker: PROTEUS
units.text: No; that it is too little.

units.speaker: TURIO
units.text: I'll wear a boot, to make it somewhat rounder.

units.speaker: JULIA
units.text:
[Aside] But love will not be spurr'd to what
it loathes.

units.speaker: TURIO
units.text: What says she to my face?

units.speaker: PROTEUS
units.text: She says it is a fair one.

units.speaker: TURIO
units.text: Nay then, the wanton lies; my face is black.

units.speaker: PROTEUS
units.text:
But pearls are fair; and the old saying is,
Black men are pearls in beauteous ladies' eyes.

units.speaker: JULIA
TURIO
units.text: How likes she my discourse?

units.speaker: PROTEUS
units.text: Ill, when you talk of war.

units.speaker: TURIO
units.text: But well, when I discourse of love and peace?

units.speaker: JULIA
units.text: [Aside] But better, indeed, when you hold your peace.

units.speaker: TURIO
units.text: What says she to my valour?

units.speaker: PROTEUS
units.text: O, sir, she makes no doubt of that.

units.speaker: JULIA
units.text: [Aside] She needs not, when she knows it cowardice.

units.speaker: TURIO
units.text: What says she to my birth?

units.speaker: PROTEUS
units.text: That you are well derived.

units.speaker: JULIA
units.text: [Aside] True; from a gentleman to a fool.

units.speaker: TURIO
units.text: Considers she my possessions?

units.speaker: PROTEUS
units.text: O, ay; and pities them.

units.speaker: TURIO
units.text: Wherefore?

units.speaker: JULIA
units.text: [Aside] That such an ass should owe them.

units.speaker: PROTEUS
units.text: That they are out by lease.

units.speaker: JULIA
units.text: Here comes the duke.
Enter DUKE

units.speaker: DUKE
units.text:
How now, Sir Proteus! How now, Turio!
Which of you saw Sir Eglamour of late?

units.speaker: TURIO
units.text: Not I.

units.speaker: PROTEUS
units.text: Nor I.

units.speaker: DUKE
units.text: Saw you my daughter?

units.speaker: PROTEUS
units.text: Neither.

units.speaker: DUKE
units.text:
Why then,
She's fled unto that peasant Valentine;
And Eglamour is in her company.
'Tis true; for Friar Laurence met them both,
As he in penance wander'd through the forest;
Him he knew well, and guess'd that it was she,
But, being mask'd, he was not sure of it;
Besides, she did intend confession
At Patrick's cell this even; and there she was not;
These likelihoods confirm her flight from hence.
Therefore, I pray you, stand not to discourse,
But mount you presently and meet with me
Upon the rising of the mountain-foot
That leads towards Mantua, whither they are fled:
Dispatch, sweet gentlemen, and follow me.
Exit

units.speaker: TURIO
units.text:
Why, this it is to be a peevish girl,
That flies her fortune when it follows her.
I'll after, more to be revenged on Eglamour
Than for the love of reckless Silvia.
Exit

units.speaker: PROTEUS
units.text:
And I will follow, more for Silvia's love
Than hate of Eglamour that goes with her.
Exit

units.speaker: JULIA
units.text:
And I will follow, more to cross that love
Than hate for Silvia that is gone for love.
Exit
Enter Outlaws with SILVIA

units.speaker: FIRST OUTLAW
units.text:
Come, come,
Be patient; we must bring you to our captain.

units.speaker: SILVIA
units.text:
A thousand more mischances than this one
Have learn'd me how to brook this patiently.

units.speaker: SECOND OUTLAW
units.text: Come, bring her away.

units.speaker: FIRST OUTLAW
units.text: Where is the gentleman that was with her?

units.speaker: THIRD OUTLAW
FIRST OUTLAW
units.text:
Come, I must bring you to our captain's cave:
Fear not; he bears an honourable mind,
And will not use a woman lawlessly.

units.speaker: SILVIA
units.text: O Valentine, this I endure for thee!
Exeunt
Enter VALENTINE.

units.speaker: VALENTINE
units.text:
How use doth breed a habit in a man!
This shadowy desert, unfrequented woods,
I better brook than flourishing peopled towns:
Here can I sit alone, unseen of any,
And to the nightingale's complaining notes
Tune my distresses and record my woes.
O thou that dost inhabit in my breast,
Leave not the mansion so long tenantless,
Lest, growing ruinous, the building fall
And leave no memory of what it was!
Repair me with thy presence, Silvia;
Thou gentle nymph, cherish thy forlorn swain!
What halloing and what stir is this to-day?
These are my mates, that make their wills their law,
Have some unhappy passenger in chase.
They love me well; yet I have much to do
To keep them from uncivil outrages.
Withdraw thee, Valentine: who's this comes here?
Enter PROTEUS, SILVIA, and JULIA

units.speaker: PROTEUS
units.text:
Madam, this service I have done for you,
Though you respect not aught your servant doth,
To hazard life and rescue you from him
That would have forced your honour and your love;
Vouchsafe me, for my meed, but one fair look;
A smaller boon than this I cannot beg
And less than this, I am sure, you cannot give.

units.speaker: VALENTINE
units.text:
[Aside] How like a dream is this I see and hear!
Love, lend me patience to forbear awhile.

units.speaker: SILVIA
units.text: O miserable, unhappy that I am!

units.speaker: PROTEUS
units.text:
Unhappy were you, madam, ere I came;
But by my coming I have made you happy.

units.speaker: SILVIA
units.text: By thy approach thou makest me most unhappy.

units.speaker: JULIA
units.text: [Aside] And me, when he approacheth to your presence.

units.speaker: SILVIA
units.text:
Had I been seized by a hungry lion,
I would have been a breakfast to the beast,
Rather than have false Proteus rescue me.
O, Heaven be judge how I love Valentine,
Whose life's as tender to me as my soul!
And full as much, for more there cannot be,
I do detest false perjured Proteus.
Therefore be gone; solicit me no more.

units.speaker: PROTEUS
units.text:
What dangerous action, stood it next to death,
Would I not undergo for one calm look!
O, 'tis the curse in love, and still approved,
When women cannot love where they're beloved!

units.speaker: SILVIA
units.text:
When Proteus cannot love where he's beloved.
Read over Julia's heart, thy first best love,
For whose dear sake thou didst then rend thy faith
Into a thousand oaths; and all those oaths
Descended into perjury, to love me.
Thou hast no faith left now, unless thou'dst two;
And that's far worse than none; better have none
Than plural faith which is too much by one:
Thou counterfeit to thy true friend!

units.speaker: PROTEUS
units.text:
In love
Who respects friend?

units.speaker: SILVIA
units.text: All men but Proteus.

units.speaker: PROTEUS
units.text:
Nay, if the gentle spirit of moving words
Can no way change you to a milder form,
I'll woo you like a soldier, at arms' end,
And love you 'gainst the nature of love,—force ye.

units.speaker: SILVIA
units.text: O heaven!

units.speaker: PROTEUS
units.text: I'll force thee yield to my desire.

units.speaker: VALENTINE
units.text:
Ruffian, let go that rude uncivil touch,
Thou friend of an ill fashion!

units.speaker: PROTEUS
units.text: Valentine!

units.speaker: VALENTINE
units.text:
Thou common friend, that's without faith or love,
For such is a friend now; treacherous man!
Thou hast beguiled my hopes; nought but mine eye
Could have persuaded me: now I dare not say
I have one friend alive; thou wouldst disprove me.
Who should be trusted, when one's own right hand
Is perjured to the bosom? Proteus,
I am sorry I must never trust thee more,
But count the world a stranger for thy sake.
The private wound is deepest: O time most accurst,
'Mongst all foes that a friend should be the worst!

units.speaker: PROTEUS
units.text:
My shame and guilt confounds me.
Forgive me, Valentine: if hearty sorrow
Be a sufficient ransom for offence,
I tender 't here; I do as truly suffer
As e'er I did commit.

units.speaker: VALENTINE
units.text:
Then I am paid;
And once again I do receive thee honest.
Who by repentance is not satisfied
Is nor of heaven nor earth, for these are pleased.
By penitence the Eternal's wrath's appeased:
And, that my love may appear plain and free,
All that was mine in Silvia I give thee.

units.speaker: JULIA
units.text: O me unhappy!

units.speaker: PROTEUS
units.text: Look to the boy.

units.speaker: VALENTINE
units.text:
Why, boy! Why, wag! How now! What's the matter?
Look up; speak.

units.speaker: JULIA
units.text:
O good sir, my master charged me to deliver a ring
to Madam Silvia, which, out of my neglect, was never
done.

units.speaker: PROTEUS
units.text: Where is that ring, boy?

units.speaker: JULIA
units.text: Here 'tis; this is it.

units.speaker: PROTEUS
units.text:
How! Let me see:
Why, this is the ring I gave to Julia.

units.speaker: JULIA
units.text:
O, cry you mercy, sir, I have mistook:
This is the ring you sent to Silvia.

units.speaker: PROTEUS
units.text:
But how camest thou by this ring? At my depart
I gave this unto Julia.

units.speaker: JULIA
units.text:
And Julia herself did give it me;
And Julia herself hath brought it hither.

units.speaker: PROTEUS
units.text: How! Julia!

units.speaker: JULIA
units.text:
Behold her that gave aim to all thy oaths,
And entertain'd 'em deeply in her heart.
How oft hast thou with perjury cleft the root!
O Proteus, let this habit make thee blush!
Be thou ashamed that I have took upon me
Such an immodest raiment, if shame live
In a disguise of love:
It is the lesser blot, modesty finds,
Women to change their shapes than men their minds.

units.speaker: PROTEUS
units.text:
Than men their minds! 'Tis true.
O heaven! Were man
But constant, he were perfect. That one error
Fills him with faults; makes him run through all the
sins:
Inconstancy falls off ere it begins.
What is in Silvia's face, but I may spy
More fresh in Julia's with a constant eye?

units.speaker: VALENTINE
PROTEUS
units.text: Bear witness, Heaven, I have my wish for ever.

units.speaker: JULIA
units.text: And I mine.
Enter Outlaws, with DUKE and TURIO

units.speaker: OUTLAWS
units.text: A prize, a prize, a prize!

units.speaker: VALENTINE
units.text:
Forbear, forbear, I say! It is my lord the duke.
Your grace is welcome to a man disgraced,
Banished Valentine.

units.speaker: DUKE
units.text: Sir Valentine!

units.speaker: TURIO
units.text: Yonder is Silvia; and Silvia's mine.

units.speaker: VALENTINE
TURIO
units.text:
Sir Valentine, I care not for her, I;
I hold him but a fool that will endanger
His body for a girl that loves him not:
I claim her not, and therefore she is thine.

units.speaker: DUKE
units.text:
The more degenerate and base art thou,
To make such means for her as thou hast done
And leave her on such slight conditions.
Now, by the honour of my ancestry,
I do applaud thy spirit, Valentine,
And think thee worthy of an empress' love:
Know then, I here forget all former griefs,
Cancel all grudge, repeal thee home again,
Plead a new state in thy unrivall'd merit,
To which I thus subscribe: Sir Valentine,
Thou art a gentleman and well derived;
Take thou thy Silvia, for thou hast deserved her.

units.speaker: VALENTINE
units.text:
I thank your grace; the gift hath made me happy.
I now beseech you, for your daughter's sake,
To grant one boom that I shall ask of you.

units.speaker: DUKE
units.text: I grant it, for thine own, whate'er it be.

units.speaker: VALENTINE
units.text:
These banish'd men that I have kept withal
Are men endued with worthy qualities:
Forgive them what they have committed here
And let them be recall'd from their exile:
They are reformed, civil, full of good
And fit for great employment, worthy lord.

units.speaker: DUKE
units.text:
Thou hast prevail'd; I pardon them and thee:
Dispose of them as thou know'st their deserts.
Come, let us go: we will include all jars
With triumphs, mirth and rare solemnity.

units.speaker: VALENTINE
units.text:
And, as we walk along, I dare be bold
With our discourse to make your grace to smile.
What think you of this page, my lord?

units.speaker: DUKE
units.text: I think the boy hath grace in him; he blushes.

units.speaker: VALENTINE
units.text: I warrant you, my lord, more grace than boy.

units.speaker: DUKE
units.text: What mean you by that saying?

units.speaker: VALENTINE
units.text:
Please you, I'll tell you as we pass along,
That you will wonder what hath fortuned.
Come, Proteus; 'tis your penance but to hear
The story of your loves discovered:
That done, our day of marriage shall be yours;
One feast, one house, one mutual happiness.
Exeunt

sentences.text: Enter VALENTINE and PROTEUS

sentences.speaker: VALENTINE
sentences.text:
Cease to persuade, my loving Proteus:
Home-keeping youth have ever homely wits.
Were't not affection chains thy tender days
To the sweet glances of thy honour'd love,
I rather would entreat thy company
To see the wonders of the world abroad,
Than, living dully sluggardized at home,
Wear out thy youth with shapeless idleness.
But since thou lovest, love still and thrive therein,
Even as I would when I to love begin.

sentences.speaker: PROTEUS
sentences.text:
Wilt thou be gone? Sweet Valentine, adieu!
Think on thy Proteus, when thou haply seest
Some rare note-worthy object in thy travel:
Wish me partaker in thy happiness
When thou dost meet good hap; and in thy danger,
If ever danger do environ thee,
Commend thy grievance to my holy prayers,
For I will be thy beadsman, Valentine.

sentences.speaker: PROTEUS
sentences.text:

When thou dost meet good hap; and in thy danger,
If ever danger do environ thee,
Commend thy grievance to my holy prayers,
For I will be thy beadsman, Valentine.

sentences.speaker: PROTEUS
sentences.text:
Wilt thou be gone? Sweet Valentine, adieu!
Think on thy Proteus, when thou haply seest
Some rare note-worthy object in thy travel:
Wish me partaker in thy happiness
When thou dost meet good hap; and in thy danger,
If ever danger do environ thee,
Commend thy grievance to my holy prayers,
For I will be thy beadsman, Valentine.

sentences.text: And on a love-book pray for my success?

sentences.speaker: PROTEUS

sentences.speaker: VALENTINE
sentences.text:
That's on some shallow story of deep love:
How young Leander cross'd the Hellespont.

sentences.speaker: PROTEUS
sentences.text:
That's a deep story of a deeper love:
For he was more than over shoes in love.

sentences.speaker: VALENTINE
sentences.text:
'Tis true; for you are over boots in love,
And yet you never swum the Hellespont.

sentences.speaker: VALENTINE

sentences.speaker: PROTEUS
sentences.text: Over the boots? Nay, give me not the boots.

sentences.speaker: PROTEUS
sentences.text: Over the boots? Nay, give me not the boots.

sentences.speaker: VALENTINE
sentences.text: No, I will not, for it boots thee not.

sentences.speaker: PROTEUS
sentences.text: What?

sentences.speaker: VALENTINE
VALENTINE
VALENTINE
VALENTINE
PROTEUS
sentences.text: So, by your circumstance, you call me fool.

sentences.speaker: VALENTINE
sentences.text: So, by your circumstance, I fear you'll prove.

sentences.speaker: PROTEUS
sentences.text: 'Tis love you cavil at: I am not Love.

sentences.speaker: PROTEUS
sentences.text: 'Tis love you cavil at: I am not Love.

sentences.speaker: VALENTINE
sentences.text:
Love is your master, for he masters you:
And he that is so yoked by a fool,
Methinks, should not be chronicled for wise.

sentences.speaker: VALENTINE
sentences.text:

Methinks, should not be chronicled for wise.

sentences.speaker: PROTEUS
sentences.text:
Yet writers say, as in the sweetest bud
The eating canker dwells, so eating love
Inhabits in the finest wits of all.

sentences.speaker: PROTEUS
sentences.text:
Yet writers say, as in the sweetest bud
The eating canker dwells, so eating love
Inhabits in the finest wits of all.

sentences.speaker: VALENTINE
sentences.text:
And writers say, as the most forward bud
Is eaten by the canker ere it blow,
Even so by love the young and tender wit
Is turn'd to folly, blasting in the bud,
Losing his verdure even in the prime
And all the fair effects of future hopes.
But wherefore waste I time to counsel thee,
That art a votary to fond desire?
Once more adieu! My father at the road
Expects my coming, there to see me shipp'd .

sentences.speaker: VALENTINE

Sweet Proteus, no; now let us take our leave.
To Milan let me hear from thee by letters
Of thy success in love, and what news else
Betideth here in absence of thy friend;
And likewise will visit thee with mine.

sentences.speaker: VALENTINE

sentences.speaker: PROTEUS
sentences.text:
He after honour hunts, I after love:
He leaves his friends to dignify them more,
I leave myself, my friends and all, for love.
Thou, Julia, thou hast metamorphosed me,
Made me neglect my studies, lose my time,
War with good counsel, set the world at nought;
Made wit with musing weak, heart sick with thought.

sentences.speaker: SPEED
sentences.text: Sir Proteus, save you! Saw you my master?

sentences.speaker: SPEED
sentences.text: Sir Proteus, save you! Saw you my master?

sentences.speaker: PROTEUS
sentences.text: But now he parted hence, to embark for Milan.

sentences.speaker: SPEED
sentences.text:
Twenty to one then he is shipp'd already,
And I have play'd the sheep in losing him.

sentences.speaker: PROTEUS
sentences.text:
Indeed, a sheep doth very often stray,
An if the shepherd be a while away.

sentences.speaker: SPEED
sentences.text:
You conclude that my master is a shepherd, then,
and I a sheep?

sentences.speaker: SPEED
sentences.text:
You conclude that my master is a shepherd, then,
and I a sheep?

sentences.speaker: PROTEUS
sentences.text: I do.

sentences.speaker: SPEED
sentences.text:
Why then, my horns are his horns, whether I wake or
sleep.

sentences.speaker: PROTEUS
sentences.text: A silly answer and fitting well a sheep.

sentences.speaker: SPEED
sentences.text: This proves me still a sheep.

sentences.speaker: PROTEUS
sentences.text: True; and thy master a shepherd.

sentences.speaker: SPEED
sentences.text: Nay, that I can deny by a circumstance.

sentences.speaker: PROTEUS
sentences.text: It shall go hard but I'll prove it by another.

sentences.speaker: SPEED
sentences.text:
The shepherd seeks the sheep, and not the sheep the
shepherd; but I seek my master, and my master seeks
not me: therefore I am no sheep.

sentences.speaker: SPEED
sentences.text:

shepherd; but I seek my master, and my master seeks

sentences.speaker: PROTEUS
sentences.text:
The sheep for fodder follow the shepherd; the
shepherd for food follows not the sheep: thou for
wages followest thy master; thy master for wages
follows not thee: therefore thou art a sheep.

Ay sir: I, a lost mutton, gave your letter to her,

lost mutton, nothing for my labour.

sentences.text: Here's too small a pasture for such store of muttons.

sentences.speaker: SPEED
sentences.text: If the ground be overcharged, you were best stick her.

sentences.speaker: PROTEUS
sentences.text: Nay: in that you are astray, 'twere best pound you.

sentences.text: Nay: in that you are astray, 'twere best pound you.

sentences.speaker: PROTEUS

sentences.speaker: PROTEUS

sentences.speaker: SPEED

You mistook, sir; I say, she did nod: and you ask
me if she did nod; and I say, 'Ay.'

sentences.speaker: PROTEUS

sentences.speaker: PROTEUS
sentences.text: Beshrew me, but you have a quick wit.

sentences.speaker: SPEED

sentences.speaker: PROTEUS
sentences.text: Well, sir, here is for your pains. What said she?

sentences.speaker: SPEED
sentences.text: Truly, sir, I think you'll hardly win her.

sentences.speaker: PROTEUS
sentences.text: Why, couldst thou perceive so much from her?

sentences.speaker: SPEED
sentences.text:
Sir, I could perceive nothing at all from her; no,
not so much as a ducat for delivering your letter:
and being so hard to me that brought your mind, I
fear she'll prove as hard to you in telling your
mind. Give her no token but stones; for she's as
hard as steel.

Go, go, be gone, to save your ship from wreck,
Which cannot perish having thee aboard,
Being destined to a drier death on shore.

I must go send some better messenger:
I fear my Julia would not deign my lines,
Receiving them from such a worthless post.

Enter JULlA and LUCETTA

sentences.speaker: JULIA
sentences.text:
But say, Lucetta, now we are alone,
Wouldst thou then counsel me to fall in love?

sentences.speaker: LUCETTA
sentences.text: Ay, madam, so you stumble not unheedfully.

sentences.speaker: LUCETTA
sentences.text: Ay, madam, so you stumble not unheedfully.

sentences.speaker: JULIA
sentences.text:
Of all the fair resort of gentlemen
That every day with parle encounter me,
In thy opinion which is worthiest love?

sentences.speaker: LUCETTA
sentences.text:
Please you repeat their names, I'll show my mind
According to my shallow simple skill.

sentences.speaker: LUCETTA
sentences.text:
Please you repeat their names, I'll show my mind
According to my shallow simple skill.

sentences.speaker: JULIA
sentences.text: What think'st thou of the fair Sir Eglamour?

sentences.speaker: LUCETTA
sentences.text:
As of a knight well-spoken, neat and fine;
But, were I you, he never should be mine.

sentences.speaker: LUCETTA
sentences.text:
As of a knight well-spoken, neat and fine;
But, were I you, he never should be mine.

sentences.speaker: JULIA
sentences.text: What think'st thou of the rich Mercatio?

sentences.speaker: LUCETTA
sentences.text: Well of his wealth; but of himself, so so.

sentences.speaker: LUCETTA
sentences.text: Well of his wealth; but of himself, so so.

sentences.speaker: LUCETTA

sentences.speaker: JULIA
sentences.text: What think'st thou of the gentle Proteus?

sentences.speaker: LUCETTA
sentences.text: Lord, Lord! To see what folly reigns in us!

sentences.speaker: LUCETTA
sentences.text: Lord, Lord! To see what folly reigns in us!

sentences.speaker: JULIA
sentences.text: How now! What means this passion at his name?

sentences.speaker: JULIA
sentences.text: How now! What means this passion at his name?

sentences.speaker: LUCETTA
sentences.text:
Pardon, dear madam: 'tis a passing shame
That I, unworthy body as I am,
Should censure thus on lovely gentlemen.

sentences.speaker: LUCETTA
sentences.text:
Pardon, dear madam: 'tis a passing shame
That I, unworthy body as I am,
Should censure thus on lovely gentlemen.

sentences.speaker: JULIA
sentences.text: Why not on Proteus, as of all the rest?

sentences.speaker: LUCETTA
sentences.text: Then thus: of many good I think him best.

sentences.speaker: JULIA
sentences.text: Your reason?

sentences.speaker: LUCETTA
sentences.text:
I have no other, but a woman's reason;
I think him so because I think him so.

sentences.speaker: LUCETTA
sentences.text:
I have no other, but a woman's reason;
I think him so because I think him so.

sentences.speaker: JULIA
sentences.text: And wouldst thou have me cast my love on him?

sentences.speaker: LUCETTA
sentences.text: Ay, if you thought your love not cast away.

sentences.speaker: JULIA
sentences.text: Why he, of all the rest, hath never moved me.

sentences.speaker: LUCETTA
sentences.text: Yet he, of all the rest, I think, best loves ye.

sentences.speaker: JULIA
sentences.text: His little speaking shows his love but small.

sentences.speaker: LUCETTA
sentences.text: Fire that's closest kept burns most of all.

sentences.speaker: JULIA
sentences.text: They do not love that do not show their love.

sentences.speaker: LUCETTA
sentences.text: O, they love least that let men know their love.

sentences.speaker: JULIA
sentences.text: I would I knew his mind.

sentences.speaker: LUCETTA
sentences.text: Peruse this paper, madam.

sentences.speaker: JULIA
sentences.text: 'To Julia.' Say, from whom?

sentences.speaker: LUCETTA
sentences.text: That the contents will show.

sentences.speaker: JULIA
sentences.text: Say, say, who gave it thee?

sentences.speaker: LUCETTA
sentences.text:
Valentine's page; and sent, I think, from Proteus.
He would have given it you; but I, being in the way,
Did in your name receive it: pardon the
fault I pray.

sentences.speaker: JULIA
sentences.text:
Now, by my modesty, a goodly broker!
Dare you presume to harbour wanton lines?
To whisper and conspire against my youth?
Now, trust me, 'tis an office of great worth
And you an officer fit for the place.
There, take the paper; see it be returned,
Or else return no more into my sight.

sentences.text:
Now, by my modesty, a goodly broker!
Dare you presume to harbour wanton lines?
To whisper and conspire against my youth?
Now, trust me, 'tis an office of great worth
And you an officer fit for the place.
There, take the paper; see it be returned,
Or else return no more into my sight.

sentences.speaker: LUCETTA
sentences.text: To plead for love deserves more fee than hate.

sentences.speaker: JULIA
sentences.text: Will ye be gone?

sentences.speaker: LUCETTA
sentences.text: That you may ruminate.
Exit

sentences.speaker: JULIA
sentences.text:
And yet I would I had o'erlooked the letter:
It were a shame to call her back again
And pray her to a fault for which I chid her.
What a fool is she, that knows I am a maid,
And would not force the letter to my view!
Since maids, in modesty, say 'no' to that
Which they would have the profferer construe 'ay.'
Fie, fie, how wayward is this foolish love
That, like a testy babe, will scratch the nurse
And presently all humbled kiss the rod!
How churlishly I chid Lucetta hence,
When willingly I would have had her here!
How angerly I taught my brow to frown,
When inward joy enforced my heart to smile!
My penance is to call Lucetta back
And ask remission for my folly past.
What ho! Lucetta!

sentences.speaker: LUCETTA
sentences.text: What would your ladyship?

sentences.speaker: JULIA
sentences.text: Is't near dinner-time?

sentences.speaker: LUCETTA
sentences.text:
I would it were,
That you might kill your stomach on your meat
And not upon your maid.

sentences.speaker: LUCETTA
sentences.text:
I would it were,
That you might kill your stomach on your meat
And not upon your maid.

sentences.speaker: JULIA
sentences.text: What is't that you took up so gingerly?

sentences.speaker: LUCETTA
sentences.text: Nothing.

sentences.speaker: JULIA
sentences.text: Why didst thou stoop, then?

sentences.speaker: LUCETTA
sentences.text: To take a paper up that I let fall.

sentences.speaker: JULIA
sentences.text: And is that paper nothing?

sentences.speaker: LUCETTA
sentences.text: Nothing concerning me.

sentences.speaker: JULIA
sentences.text: Then let it lie for those that it concerns.

sentences.speaker: LUCETTA
sentences.text:
Madam, it will not lie where it concerns
Unless it have a false interpeter.

sentences.speaker: JULIA
sentences.text: Some love of yours hath writ to you in rhyme.

sentences.speaker: LUCETTA
sentences.text:
That I might sing it, madam, to a tune.
Give me a note: your ladyship can set.

sentences.speaker: LUCETTA
sentences.text:
That I might sing it, madam, to a tune.
Give me a note: your ladyship can set.

sentences.speaker: JULIA
sentences.text:
As little by such toys as may be possible.
Best sing it to the tune of 'Light o' love.'

sentences.speaker: JULIA
sentences.text:
As little by such toys as may be possible.
Best sing it to the tune of 'Light o' love.'

sentences.speaker: LUCETTA
sentences.text: It is too heavy for so light a tune.

sentences.speaker: JULIA
sentences.text: Heavy! Belike it hath some burden then?

sentences.speaker: JULIA
sentences.text: Heavy! Belike it hath some burden then?

sentences.speaker: LUCETTA
sentences.text: Ay, and melodious were it, would you sing it.

sentences.speaker: LUCETTA
sentences.text: Ay, and melodious were it, would you sing it.

sentences.speaker: JULIA
sentences.text: And why not you?

sentences.speaker: LUCETTA
sentences.text: I cannot reach so high.

sentences.speaker: JULIA
sentences.text: Let's see your song. How now, minion!

sentences.speaker: JULIA
sentences.text: Let's see your song. How now, minion!

sentences.speaker: LUCETTA
sentences.text:
Keep tune there still, so you will sing it out:
And yet methinks I do not like this tune.

sentences.speaker: LUCETTA
sentences.text:
Keep tune there still, so you will sing it out:
And yet methinks I do not like this tune.

sentences.speaker: JULIA
sentences.text: You do not?

sentences.speaker: LUCETTA
sentences.text: No, madam; it is too sharp.

sentences.speaker: LUCETTA
sentences.text: No, madam; it is too sharp.

sentences.speaker: JULIA
sentences.text: You, minion, are too saucy.

sentences.speaker: LUCETTA
sentences.text:
Nay, now you are too flat
And mar the concord with too harsh a descant:
There wanteth but a mean to fill your song.

sentences.speaker: LUCETTA
sentences.text:
Nay, now you are too flat
And mar the concord with too harsh a descant:
There wanteth but a mean to fill your song.

sentences.speaker: JULIA
sentences.text: The mean is drown'd with your unruly bass.

sentences.speaker: LUCETTA
sentences.text: Indeed, I bid the base for Proteus.

sentences.speaker: JULIA
sentences.text:
This babble shall not henceforth trouble me.
Here is a coil with protestation!

sentences.speaker: JULIA
sentences.text:
This babble shall not henceforth trouble me.
Here is a coil with protestation!

sentences.speaker: JULIA
sentences.text:
This babble shall not henceforth trouble me.
Here is a coil with protestation!

sentences.speaker: JULIA
sentences.text:
Go get you gone, and let the papers lie:
You would be fingering them, to anger me.

sentences.speaker: JULIA
sentences.text:
Go get you gone, and let the papers lie:
You would be fingering them, to anger me.

sentences.speaker: LUCETTA
sentences.text:
She makes it strange; but she would be best pleased
To be so anger'd with another letter.

sentences.speaker: LUCETTA
sentences.text:
She makes it strange; but she would be best pleased
To be so anger'd with another letter.
Exit

sentences.speaker: JULIA
sentences.text:
Nay, would I were so anger'd with the same!
O hateful hands, to tear such loving words!
Injurious wasps, to feed on such sweet honey
And kill the bees that yield it with your stings!
I'll kiss each several paper for amends.
Look, here is writ 'kind Julia.' Unkind Julia!
As in revenge of thy ingratitude,
I throw thy name against the bruising stones,
Trampling contemptuously on thy disdain.
And here is writ 'love-wounded Proteus.'
Poor wounded name! My bosom as a bed
Shall lodge thee till thy wound be thoroughly heal'd;
And thus I search it with a sovereign kiss.
But twice or thrice was 'Proteus' written down.
Be calm, good wind, blow not a word away
Till I have found each letter in the letter,
Except mine own name: that some whirlwind bear
Unto a ragged fearful-hanging rock
And throw it thence into the raging sea!
Lo, here in one line is his name twice writ,
'Poor forlorn Proteus, passionate Proteus,
To the sweet Julia:' that I'll tear away.
And yet I will not, sith so prettily
He couples it to his complaining names.
Thus will I fold them one on another:
Now kiss, embrace, contend, do what you will.

And here is writ 'love-wounded Proteus.'
Poor wounded name! My bosom as a bed
Shall lodge thee till thy wound be thoroughly heal'd;
And thus I search it with a sovereign kiss.
But twice or thrice was 'Proteus' written down.
Be calm, good wind, blow not a word away
Till I have found each letter in the letter,
Except mine own name: that some whirlwind bear
Unto a ragged fearful-hanging rock
And throw it thence into the raging sea!
Lo, here in one line is his name twice writ,
'Poor forlorn Proteus, passionate Proteus,
To the sweet Julia:' that I'll tear away.
And yet I will not, sith so prettily
He couples it to his complaining names.
Thus will I fold them one on another:
Now kiss, embrace, contend, do what you will.

sentences.speaker: JULIA
sentences.text:
Nay, would I were so anger'd with the same!
O hateful hands, to tear such loving words!
Injurious wasps, to feed on such sweet honey
And kill the bees that yield it with your stings!
I'll kiss each several paper for amends.
Look, here is writ 'kind Julia.' Unkind Julia!
As in revenge of thy ingratitude,
I throw thy name against the bruising stones,
Trampling contemptuously on thy disdain.
And here is writ 'love-wounded Proteus.'
Poor wounded name! My bosom as a bed
Shall lodge thee till thy wound be thoroughly heal'd;
And thus I search it with a sovereign kiss.
But twice or thrice was 'Proteus' written down.

Till I have found each letter in the letter,
Except mine own name: that some whirlwind bear

And throw it thence into the raging sea!

'Poor forlorn Proteus, passionate Proteus,
To the sweet Julia:' that I'll tear away.
And yet I will not, sith so prettily
He couples it to his complaining names.
Thus will I fold them one on another:
Now kiss, embrace, contend, do what you will.

Re-enter LUCETTA

sentences.speaker: LUCETTA
sentences.text:
Madam,
Dinner is ready, and your father stays.

sentences.speaker: JULIA
sentences.text: Well, let us go.

sentences.speaker: LUCETTA
sentences.text: What, shall these papers lie like tell-tales here?

sentences.speaker: LUCETTA
sentences.text: What, shall these papers lie like tell-tales here?

sentences.speaker: LUCETTA
sentences.text: What, shall these papers lie like tell-tales here?

sentences.speaker: JULIA
sentences.text: If you respect them, best to take them up.

sentences.speaker: LUCETTA
sentences.text:
Nay, I was taken up for laying them down:
Yet here they shall not lie, for catching cold.

sentences.speaker: LUCETTA
sentences.text:
Nay, I was taken up for laying them down:
Yet here they shall not lie, for catching cold.

sentences.speaker: JULIA
sentences.text: I see you have a month's mind to them.

sentences.speaker: LUCETTA
sentences.text:
Ay, madam, you may say what sights you see;
I see things too, although you judge I wink.

sentences.speaker: LUCETTA
sentences.text:
Ay, madam, you may say what sights you see;
I see things too, although you judge I wink.

sentences.speaker: JULIA
sentences.text: Come, come; will't please you go?

sentences.speaker: JULIA
sentences.text: Come, come; will't please you go?
Exeunt
Enter ANTONIO and PANTHINO

sentences.speaker: ANTONIO
sentences.text:
Tell me, Panthino, what sad talk was that
Wherewith my brother held you in the cloister?

sentences.speaker: PANTHINO
sentences.text: 'Twas of his nephew Proteus, your son.

sentences.speaker: ANTONIO
sentences.text: Why, what of him?

sentences.speaker: PANTHINO
sentences.text:
He wonder'd that your lordship
Would suffer him to spend his youth at home,
While other men, of slender reputation,
Put forth their sons to seek preferment out:
Some to the wars, to try their fortune there;
Some to discover islands far away;
Some to the studious universities.
For any or for all these exercises,
He said that Proteus your son was meet,
And did request me to importune you
To let him spend his time no more at home,
Which would be great impeachment to his age,
In having known no travel in his youth.

sentences.speaker: ANTONIO
sentences.text:
Nor need'st thou much importune me to that
Whereon this month I have been hammering.
I have consider'd well his loss of time
And how he cannot be a perfect man,
Not being tried and tutor'd in the world:
Experience is by industry achieved
And perfected by the swift course of time.
Then tell me, whither were I best to send him?

sentences.speaker: PANTHINO
sentences.text:
I think your lordship is not ignorant
How his companion, youthful Valentine,
Attends the emperor in his royal court.

sentences.speaker: ANTONIO
sentences.text: I know it well.

sentences.speaker: PANTHINO
sentences.text:
'Twere good, I think, your lordship sent him thither:
There shall he practise tilts and tournaments,
Hear sweet discourse, converse with noblemen.
And be in eye of every exercise
Worthy his youth and nobleness of birth.

sentences.speaker: ANTONIO
sentences.text:
I like thy counsel; well hast thou advised:
And that thou mayst perceive how well I like it,
The execution of it shall make known.
Even with the speediest expedition
I will dispatch him to the emperor's court.

sentences.speaker: PANTHINO
sentences.text:
To-morrow, may it please you, Don Alphonso,
With other gentlemen of good esteem,
Are journeying to salute the emperor
And to commend their service to his will.

sentences.speaker: PANTHINO

sentences.speaker: ANTONIO
sentences.text:
Good company; with them shall Proteus go:
And, in good time! Now will we break with him.

Enter PROTEUS

sentences.speaker: PROTEUS
sentences.text:
Sweet love! Sweet lines! Sweet life!
Here is her hand, the agent of her heart;
Here is her oath for love, her honour's pawn.
O, that our fathers would applaud our loves,
To seal our happiness with their consents!
O heavenly Julia!

sentences.speaker: ANTONIO
sentences.text: How now! What letter are you reading there?

sentences.speaker: ANTONIO
sentences.text: How now! What letter are you reading there?

sentences.speaker: PROTEUS
sentences.text:
May't please your lordship, 'tis a word or two
Of commendations sent from Valentine,
Deliver'd by a friend that came from him.

sentences.speaker: ANTONIO
sentences.text: Lend me the letter; let me see what news.

sentences.speaker: ANTONIO
sentences.text: Lend me the letter; let me see what news.

sentences.speaker: PROTEUS
sentences.text:
There is no news, my lord, but that he writes
How happily he lives, how well beloved
And daily graced by the emperor;
Wishing me with him, partner of his fortune.

sentences.speaker: PROTEUS
sentences.text:

How happily he lives, how well beloved

Wishing me with him, partner of his fortune.

Wishing me with him, partner of his fortune.

sentences.speaker: ANTONIO
sentences.text: And how stand you affected to his wish?

sentences.speaker: PROTEUS
sentences.text:
As one relying on your lordship's will
And not depending on his friendly wish.

sentences.speaker: PROTEUS
sentences.text:
As one relying on your lordship's will
And not depending on his friendly wish.

sentences.speaker: ANTONIO
sentences.text:
My will is something sorted with his wish.
Muse not that I thus suddenly proceed;
For what I will, I will, and there an end.
I am resolved that thou shalt spend some time
With Valentinus in the emperor's court:
What maintenance he from his friends receives,
Like exhibition thou shalt have from me.
To-morrow be in readiness to go:
Excuse it not, for I am peremptory.

sentences.speaker: PROTEUS
sentences.text:
My lord, I cannot be so soon provided:
Please you, deliberate a day or two.

sentences.speaker: PROTEUS
sentences.text:
My lord, I cannot be so soon provided:
Please you, deliberate a day or two.

sentences.speaker: ANTONIO
sentences.text:
Look, what thou want'st shall be sent after thee:
No more of stay! To-morrow thou must go.
Come on, Panthino: you shall be employ'd
To hasten on his expedition.

sentences.speaker: PROTEUS
sentences.text:
Thus have I shunn'd the fire for fear of burning,
And drench'd me in the sea, where I am drown'd.
I fear'd to show my father Julia's letter,
Lest he should take exceptions to my love;
And with the vantage of mine own excuse
Hath he excepted most against my love.
O, how this spring of love resembleth
The uncertain glory of an April day,
Which now shows all the beauty of the sun,
And by and by a cloud takes all away!

sentences.speaker: PANTHINO
sentences.text:
Sir Proteus, your father calls for you:
He is in haste; therefore, I pray you to go.

sentences.speaker: PANTHINO
sentences.text:
Sir Proteus, your father calls for you:
He is in haste; therefore, I pray you to go.

sentences.speaker: PROTEUS
sentences.text:
Why, this it is: my heart accords thereto,
And yet a thousand times it answers 'no.'

sentences.speaker: VALENTINE
sentences.text: How now, sirrah?

sentences.speaker: SPEED
sentences.text: She is not within hearing, sir.

sentences.speaker: VALENTINE
sentences.text: Why, sir, who bade you call her?

sentences.speaker: SPEED

sentences.speaker: SPEED
sentences.text: Your worship, sir; or else I mistook.

sentences.speaker: VALENTINE
sentences.text: Well, you'll still be too forward.

sentences.speaker: SPEED
sentences.text: And yet I was last chidden for being too slow.

sentences.speaker: VALENTINE
sentences.text: Go to, sir: tell me, do you know Madam Silvia?

sentences.speaker: VALENTINE
sentences.text: Go to, sir: tell me, do you know Madam Silvia?

sentences.speaker: SPEED
sentences.text: She that your worship loves?

sentences.speaker: VALENTINE
sentences.text: Why, how know you that I am in love?

sentences.speaker: SPEED
sentences.text:
Marry, by these special marks: first, you have
learned, like Sir Proteus, to wreathe your arms,
like a malecontent; to relish a love-song, like a
robin-redbreast; to walk alone, like one that had
the pestilence; to sigh, like a school-boy that had
lost his A B C; to weep, like a young wench that had
buried her grandam; to fast, like one that takes
diet; to watch like one that fears robbing; to
speak puling, like a beggar at Hallowmas. You were
wont, when you laughed, to crow like a cock; when you
walked, to walk like one of the lions; when you
fasted, it was presently after dinner; when you
looked sadly, it was for want of money: and now you
are metamorphosed with a mistress, that, when I look
on you, I can hardly think you my master .

sentences.speaker: VALENTINE
sentences.text: Are all these things perceived in me?

sentences.speaker: SPEED
sentences.text: They are all perceived without ye.

sentences.speaker: VALENTINE
sentences.text: Without me? They cannot.

sentences.speaker: VALENTINE
sentences.text: Without me? They cannot.

sentences.speaker: SPEED
sentences.text:
Without you? Nay, that's certain, for, without you
were so simple, none else would: but you are so
without these follies, that these follies are within
you and shine through you like the water in an
urinal, that not an eye that sees you but is a
physician to comment on your malady.

sentences.speaker: SPEED
sentences.text: She that you gaze on so as she sits at supper?

sentences.speaker: VALENTINE
sentences.text: Hast thou observed that? Even she, I mean.

sentences.speaker: VALENTINE

sentences.speaker: SPEED
sentences.text: Is she not hard-favoured, sir?

sentences.speaker: VALENTINE
sentences.text: Not so fair, boy, as well-favoured.

sentences.speaker: SPEED
sentences.text: Sir, I know that well enough.

sentences.speaker: VALENTINE
sentences.text: What dost thou know?

sentences.speaker: SPEED
sentences.text: That she is not so fair as, of you, well-favoured.

sentences.speaker: SPEED
sentences.text: That she is not so fair as, of you, well-favoured.

sentences.speaker: VALENTINE
sentences.text:
I mean that her beauty is exquisite, but her favour
infinite.

sentences.speaker: SPEED
sentences.text:
That's because the one is painted and the other out
of all count.

sentences.speaker: VALENTINE
sentences.text: How painted? And how out of count?

sentences.speaker: SPEED
sentences.text:
Marry, sir, so painted, to make her fair, that no
man counts of her beauty.

sentences.speaker: VALENTINE
sentences.text: How esteemest thou me? I account of her beauty.

sentences.speaker: VALENTINE
sentences.text: How esteemest thou me? I account of her beauty.

sentences.text: You never saw her since she was deformed.

sentences.speaker: SPEED

sentences.text:
I have loved her ever since I saw her; and still I
see her beautiful.

sentences.speaker: VALENTINE

I have loved her ever since I saw her; and still I

sentences.text: If you love her, you cannot see her.

sentences.speaker: SPEED
sentences.text:
Because Love is blind. O, that you had mine eyes;
or your own eyes had the lights they were wont to
have when you chid at Sir Proteus for going
ungartered!

O jest unseen, inscrutable, invisible,
As a nose on a man's face, or a weathercock on a
steeple!
My master sues to her, and she hath
taught her suitor,
He being her pupil, to become her tutor.
O excellent device! Was there ever heard a better,
That my master, being scribe, to himself should write
the letter?

sentences.text: How now, sir? What are you reasoning with yourself?

sentences.speaker: VALENTINE

sentences.speaker: SPEED

sentences.speaker: VALENTINE
sentences.text: To do what?

sentences.speaker: SPEED
sentences.text: To be a spokesman for Madam Silvia.

sentences.speaker: VALENTINE
sentences.text: To whom?

sentences.speaker: SPEED
sentences.text: To yourself: why, she wooes you by a figure.

sentences.speaker: SPEED
sentences.text: To yourself: why, she wooes you by a figure.

sentences.speaker: VALENTINE

sentences.speaker: SPEED
sentences.text: By a letter, I should say.

sentences.speaker: VALENTINE
sentences.text: Why, she hath not writ to me?

sentences.speaker: SPEED
sentences.text:
What need she, when she hath made you write to
yourself? Why, do you not perceive the jest?

sentences.speaker: SPEED

her earnest?

sentences.speaker: SPEED
sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: VALENTINE
sentences.text: She gave me none, except an angry word.

sentences.speaker: SPEED
sentences.text: Why, she hath given you a letter.

sentences.speaker: VALENTINE
sentences.text: That's the letter I writ to her friend.

sentences.speaker: SPEED
sentences.text: And that letter hath she delivered, and there an end.

sentences.speaker: SPEED
sentences.text: And that letter hath she delivered, and there an end.

sentences.speaker: VALENTINE
sentences.text: I would it were no worse.

sentences.speaker: SPEED
sentences.text:
I'll warrant you, 'tis as well:
For often have you writ to her, and she, in modesty,
Or else for want of idle time, could not again reply;
Or fearing else some messenger that might her mind
discover,
Herself hath taught her love himself to write unto her
lover.
All this I speak in print, for in print I found it.
Why muse you, sir? 'Tis dinner-time.

your mistress; be moved, be moved.

sentences.speaker: LANCE
sentences.text:
Nay, 'twill be this hour ere I have done weeping;
all the kind of the Lances have this very fault. I
have received my proportion, like the prodigious
son, and am going with Sir Proteus to the Imperial's
court. I think Crab, my dog, be the sourest-natured
dog that lives: my mother weeping, my father
wailing, my sister crying, our maid howling, our cat
wringing her hands, and all our house in a great
perplexity, yet did not this cruel-hearted cur shed
one tear: he is a stone, a very pebble stone, and
has no more pity in him than a dog: a Jew would have
wept to have seen our parting; why, my grandam,
having no eyes, look you, wept herself blind at my
parting. Nay, I'll show you the manner of it. This
shoe is my father: no, this left shoe is my father:
no, no, this left shoe is my mother: nay, that
cannot be so neither: yes, it is so, it is so, it
hath the worser sole. This shoe, with the hole in
it, is my mother, and this my father; a vengeance
on't! There 'tis: now, sit, this staff is my
sister, for, look you, she is as white as a lily and
as small as a wand: this hat is Nan, our maid: I
am the dog: no, the dog is himself, and I am the
dog—Oh! The dog is me, and I am myself; ay, so,
so. Now come I to my father; Father, your blessing:
now should not the shoe speak a word for weeping:
now should I kiss my father; well, he weeps on. Now
come I to my mother: O, that she could speak now
like a wood woman! Well, I kiss her; why, there
'tis; here's my mother's breath up and down. Now
come I to my sister; mark the moan she makes. Now
the dog all this while sheds not a tear nor speaks a
word; but see how I lay the dust with my tears.

no, no, this left shoe is my mother: nay, that
cannot be so neither: yes, it is so, it is so, it
hath the worser sole. This shoe, with the hole in
it, is my mother, and this my father; a vengeance
on't! There 'tis: now, sit, this staff is my
sister, for, look you, she is as white as a lily and
as small as a wand: this hat is Nan, our maid: I
am the dog: no, the dog is himself, and I am the
dog—Oh! The dog is me, and I am myself; ay, so,
so. Now come I to my father; Father, your blessing:
now should not the shoe speak a word for weeping:
now should I kiss my father; well, he weeps on. Now
come I to my mother: O, that she could speak now
like a wood woman! Well, I kiss her; why, there
'tis; here's my mother's breath up and down. Now
come I to my sister; mark the moan she makes. Now
the dog all this while sheds not a tear nor speaks a
word; but see how I lay the dust with my tears.

Enter PANTHINO

sentences.speaker: PANTHINO
sentences.text:
Launce, away, away, aboard! Thy master is shipped
and thou art to post after with oars. What's the
matter? Why weepest thou, man? Away, ass! You'll
lose the tide, if you tarry any longer.

sentences.speaker: PANTHINO
sentences.text:

and thou art to post after with oars. What's the
matter? Why weepest thou, man? Away, ass! You'll
lose the tide, if you tarry any longer.

sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: LANCE
sentences.text:
It is no matter if the tied were lost; for it is the
unkindest tied that ever any man tied.

sentences.speaker: PANTHINO
sentences.text: What's the unkindest tide?

sentences.speaker: LANCE
sentences.text: Why, he that's tied here, Crab, my dog.

sentences.speaker: LANCE
sentences.text: Why, he that's tied here, Crab, my dog.

sentences.speaker: PANTHINO
sentences.text:
Tut, man, I mean thou'lt lose the flood, and, in
losing the flood, lose thy voyage, and, in losing
thy voyage, lose thy master, and, in losing thy
master, lose thy service, and, in losing thy
service,—Why dost thou stop my mouth?

sentences.speaker: PANTHINO
sentences.text:
Tut, man, I mean thou'lt lose the flood, and, in
losing the flood, lose thy voyage, and, in losing
thy voyage, lose thy master, and, in losing thy
master, lose thy service, and, in losing thy
service,—Why dost thou stop my mouth?

sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: PANTHINO
sentences.text:
Tut, man, I mean thou'lt lose the flood, and, in
losing the flood, lose thy voyage, and, in losing
thy voyage, lose thy master, and, in losing thy
master, lose thy service, and, in losing thy
service,—Why dost thou stop my mouth?

sentences.speaker: LANCE
sentences.text: For fear thou shouldst lose thy tongue.

sentences.speaker: PANTHINO
sentences.text: Where should I lose my tongue?

sentences.speaker: LANCE
sentences.text: In thy tale.

sentences.speaker: PANTHINO
sentences.text: In thy tail!

sentences.speaker: LANCE
sentences.text:
Lose the tide, and the voyage, and the master, and
the service, and the tied! Why, man, if the river
were dry, I am able to fill it with my tears; if the
wind were down, I could drive the boat with my sighs.

sentences.speaker: LANCE
sentences.text:

the service, and the tied! Why, man, if the river
were dry, I am able to fill it with my tears; if the
wind were down, I could drive the boat with my sighs.

sentences.speaker: PANTHINO
sentences.text: Come, come away, man; I was sent to call thee.

sentences.speaker: PANTHINO
sentences.text: Come, come away, man; I was sent to call thee.

sentences.speaker: LANCE
sentences.text: Sir, call me what thou darest.

sentences.speaker: PANTHINO
sentences.text: Wilt thou go?

sentences.speaker: LANCE
sentences.text: Well, I will go.
Exeunt
Enter SILVIA, VALENTINE, TURIO, and SPEED

sentences.speaker: SILVIA
sentences.text: Servant!

sentences.speaker: VALENTINE
sentences.text: Mistress?

sentences.speaker: SPEED
sentences.text: Master, Sir Turio frowns on you.

sentences.speaker: VALENTINE
sentences.text: Ay, boy, it's for love.

sentences.speaker: VALENTINE
sentences.text: Ay, boy, it's for love.

sentences.speaker: SPEED
sentences.text: Not of you.

sentences.speaker: VALENTINE
sentences.text: Of my mistress, then.

sentences.speaker: SPEED
sentences.text: 'Twere good you knocked him.
Exit

sentences.speaker: SILVIA
sentences.text: Servant, you are sad.

sentences.speaker: VALENTINE
sentences.text: Indeed, madam, I seem so.

sentences.speaker: TURIO
sentences.text: Seem you that you are not?

sentences.speaker: VALENTINE
sentences.text: Haply I do.

sentences.speaker: TURIO
sentences.text: So do counterfeits.

sentences.speaker: VALENTINE
sentences.text: So do you.

sentences.speaker: TURIO
sentences.text: What seem I that I am not?

sentences.speaker: VALENTINE
sentences.text: Wise.

sentences.speaker: TURIO
sentences.text: What instance of the contrary?

sentences.speaker: VALENTINE
sentences.text: Your folly.

sentences.speaker: TURIO
sentences.text: And how quote you my folly?

sentences.speaker: VALENTINE
sentences.text: I quote it in your jerkin.

sentences.speaker: TURIO
sentences.text: My jerkin is a doublet.

sentences.speaker: VALENTINE
sentences.text: Well, then, I'll double your folly.

sentences.speaker: TURIO
sentences.text: How?

sentences.speaker: SILVIA
sentences.text: What, angry, Sir Turio! Do you change colour?

sentences.speaker: SILVIA
sentences.text: What, angry, Sir Turio! Do you change colour?

sentences.speaker: VALENTINE
sentences.text: Give him leave, madam; he is a kind of chameleon.

sentences.speaker: VALENTINE
sentences.text: Give him leave, madam; he is a kind of chameleon.

sentences.speaker: TURIO
sentences.text:
That hath more mind to feed on your blood than live
in your air.

sentences.speaker: VALENTINE
sentences.text: You have said, sir.

sentences.speaker: TURIO
sentences.text: Ay, sir, and done too, for this time.

sentences.speaker: TURIO
sentences.text: Ay, sir, and done too, for this time.

sentences.speaker: VALENTINE
sentences.text: I know it well, sir; you always end ere you begin.

sentences.speaker: VALENTINE
sentences.text: I know it well, sir; you always end ere you begin.

sentences.speaker: SILVIA
sentences.text:
A fine volley of words, gentlemen, and quickly shot
off.

sentences.speaker: SILVIA
sentences.text:
A fine volley of words, gentlemen, and quickly shot
off.

sentences.speaker: VALENTINE
sentences.text: 'Tis indeed, madam; we thank the giver.

sentences.speaker: VALENTINE
sentences.text: 'Tis indeed, madam; we thank the giver.

sentences.speaker: SILVIA
sentences.text: Who is that, servant?

sentences.speaker: VALENTINE
sentences.text:
Yourself, sweet lady; for you gave the fire. Sir
Turio borrows his wit from your ladyship's looks,
and spends what he borrows kindly in your company.

sentences.speaker: VALENTINE
sentences.text:
Yourself, sweet lady; for you gave the fire. Sir
Turio borrows his wit from your ladyship's looks,
and spends what he borrows kindly in your company.

sentences.speaker: VALENTINE
sentences.text:
Yourself, sweet lady; for you gave the fire. Sir
Turio borrows his wit from your ladyship's looks,
and spends what he borrows kindly in your company.

sentences.speaker: TURIO
sentences.text:
Sir, if you spend word for word with me, I shall
make your wit bankrupt.

sentences.speaker: VALENTINE
sentences.text:
I know it well, sir; you have an exchequer of words,
and, I think, no other treasure to give your
followers, for it appears by their bare liveries,
that they live by your bare words.

sentences.speaker: VALENTINE
sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: SILVIA
sentences.text: No more, gentlemen, no more:—here comes my father.

sentences.speaker: SILVIA

Enter DUKE

sentences.speaker: DUKE
sentences.text:
Now, daughter Silvia, you are hard beset.
Sir Valentine, your father's in good health:
What say you to a letter from your friends
Of much good news?
